title: practical english usage - Part 16
content:
vowel  the letters a, e, i, o, u and their combinations, and their usual sounds been forgotten.

verb phrase  a verbal structure that has more than one part. Example: would have states. See also auxiliary verb, modal auxiliary verb, verb phrase.

verb  a word like ask, wake, play, be, can. Most verbs refer to actions, events or used with the article a/an. Examples: mud; rudeness; furniture.

uncountable noun  a noun which  has no plural form and cannot normally be Phonetic alphabet •  xxx The  sign (’ ) shows stress (►   313).

letters of the phonetic alphabet used in this book, with examples of the words to represent all the sounds of the language. The  following list contains all the words, because the ordinary English alphabet does not have enough letters It is necessary to use a special alphabet to show the pronunciation of English Phonetic      alphabet Vocabulary        Sections      29-31: entries      321-635 Grammar      Sections      1-28: entries      1-320 Practical      English     Usage grammar •  Section 1  Verbs One thinks that something will happen, but one does not want that it will happen.

somebody to see the doctor, but one does not suggest somebody to see-the-doetor.

hopes to see somebody, but one looks forward to seeing somebody. One advises can tell somebody something, but one cannot explain somebody something. One meaning. Before an object, wait is followed by for, expect has no preposition. One verb. It is also partly a matter of grammatical rules that have nothing to do with instance, it is normal to expect a noun; after try or stop, it is natural to expect a structures. This is partly a matter of meaning: after a verb like eat or break, for Different non-auxiliary verbs can be followed by different kinds of words and What    can   follow     a  verb?

probability, obligation and permission. For details, ►    Section 7.

and ought) are used with other verbs to add ideas such as futurity, certainty, Modal  auxiliary verbs (must, can, could, will, would, may, might, shall, should structures, questions and negatives (see below and ►    Section 2).
